skill.name: using-git-worktrees
skill.description: Guides agents through creating isolated Git worktrees, choosing a safe directory, running setup, and verifying a clean test baseline.
skill.summary: Create isolated Git worktrees for feature work with directory checks and baseline verification.
skill.author: Cycleaddict

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer found many command and filesystem patterns, but most are documentation examples for legitimate Git worktree use. The confirmed risk is that the skill instructs agents to run dependency installation, build, and test commands, which can execute repository-controlled scripts.

medium_findings.title: Repository-Controlled Setup And Test Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill tells agents to run package installation, build, and test commands after creating a worktree. This is normal for development workflows, but these commands can execute scripts from an untrusted repository.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly documented and include npm install, cargo build, pip install, poetry install, and test runners. The risk is contextual rather than malicious because the skill is about setting up development worktrees.

low_findings.title: Command Examples Are Legitimate Worktree Operations
low_findings.description: The many shell command detections are examples for listing directories, checking ignore rules, creating a Git worktree, and changing into it. Arguments are generally quoted, and no evidence of exfiltration or destructive behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are directly related to Git worktree setup and do not show hidden execution, network access, or credential handling. They remain a low concern because agents may execute them in user repositories.

low_findings.title: Hidden Directory And Home Path Usage Is Benign
low_findings.description: References to .worktrees and ~/.config/superpowers/worktrees describe optional storage locations for worktrees. The skill does not instruct reading secrets or hidden credentials.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hidden paths are used as worktree directories, not as sensitive data sources. The context clearly shows workspace organization rather than covert filesystem access.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detection Is A False Positive
low_findings.description: The static weak cryptography alerts appear to match ordinary words in the description and integration section. No cryptographic API, hash operation, or encryption routine appears in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is frontmatter description text and line 212 refers to design approval. There is no cryptographic code or instruction on either line.

dangerous_patterns.title: Automatic Dependency And Test Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: Running setup and test commands can execute repository scripts in package managers, build systems, or test frameworks. Users should apply this skill only in repositories they trust or after reviewing project scripts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicitly present and commonly capable of executing project code. The skill frames it as setup and validation, so the risk is elevated but not evidence of malware.

content.value_statement: Feature work often needs isolation from an active workspace. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through directory selection, worktree creation, setup, and baseline checks.
actual_capabilities: Chooses an existing .worktrees or worktrees directory when present.
Checks CLAUDE.md for project-specific worktree directory preferences.
Prompts the user when the worktree location is ambiguous.
Verifies project-local worktree directories are ignored by Git.
Creates a new Git worktree and branch for the requested work.
Runs common setup and test commands based on detected project files.
limitations: It is a procedural guide, not an executable worktree manager.
It assumes Git is available and the repository supports worktrees.
Setup and test commands may execute project-defined scripts.
It does not resolve dependency failures or test failures automatically.

use_cases.title: Isolate Feature Development
use_cases.description: Create a clean worktree before starting a feature so current edits and branches stay untouched.

use_cases.title: Prepare Agent Implementation Work
use_cases.description: Give an AI coding agent a repeatable process for creating a safe workspace before modifying files.

use_cases.title: Standardize Repository Workflow
use_cases.description: Use consistent worktree locations and baseline checks across teams and projects.

prompt_templates.title: Create A Worktree
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for one feature branch.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the using-git-worktrees skill to create an isolated worktree for my next feature. Ask before choosing a location if the repository has no convention.

prompt_templates.title: Follow Project Conventions
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up a Git worktree for this task. Check existing worktree directories and CLAUDE.md before creating anything.

prompt_templates.title: Verify Before Implementation
prompt_templates.scenario: Prepare a workspace before code changes.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a worktree for the planned implementation, run the detected setup steps, and report the baseline test result before editing files.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Worktree Readiness
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced validation of a worktree workflow.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the worktree skill to prepare an isolated branch, confirm the worktree directory is ignored, run setup and tests, and list any risks before proceeding.

output_examples.input: Set up an isolated workspace for an authentication feature.
output_examples.output: The assistant reports the selected worktree directory, confirms the ignore check, creates the feature branch, runs setup, and summarizes test status.

output_examples.input: The repository has no .worktrees directory or CLAUDE.md preference.
output_examples.output: The assistant asks the user to choose between a project-local worktree directory and a global configuration directory.
The assistant reports the failing test command and asks whether to investigate or proceed with the known failure.

best_practices: Use this skill before starting implementation that should not disturb the current branch.
Review package scripts before running setup in repositories you do not trust.
Keep the worktree directory ignored when it lives inside the project.
anti_patterns: Do not create project-local worktrees without verifying Git ignore behavior.
Do not run setup commands blindly in untrusted repositories.
Do not continue after failing baseline tests without user approval.

faq.question: Does this skill create worktrees automatically?
faq.answer: It provides a procedure for the agent to follow. The agent still runs the Git commands in the active environment.

faq.question: Why does it check .worktrees before worktrees?
faq.answer: The skill prefers the hidden project-local directory when both options exist.

faq.question: Why is Git ignore verification required?
faq.answer: It helps prevent worktree contents from appearing as tracked files in the main repository.

faq.question: Can setup commands be risky?
faq.answer: Yes. Package installation, builds, and tests can run project-defined scripts, especially in untrusted repositories.

faq.question: What happens when tests fail?
faq.answer: The skill tells the agent to report the failure and ask before proceeding.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report marks it as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
